Possible factors that affect aiming speed

Introduction

In my project 2 I decided to explore whether or not hunger had an effect on the speed in which I could complete the HumanBenchmark aim speed test, where you have to click on 30 targets as quickly as possible. For this project I decided to explore whether other factors such as the time of day, location the test was taken, and amount of sleep as well as hunger also played a part in my aim speed. I collected my data through observational logging on a Google form. I recorded my data using the same structure as project 2 where I would fill out the form within 30 minutes before having something to eat and within 30 minutes after eating.

Hunger and Aim performance

Scatterplot showing the relationship of hunger level and aim speed. The hungrier I tended to be, the worse my aim speed.

This visualization shows the relationship between hunger level and aim speed in milliseconds (ms). The hunger level is measured from 1-5 where 1 is very hungry and 5 is not hungry at all. The data suggests that reaction speed can be quite variable across all of the hunger levels, however it is clear that the times taken to complete the aim speed test tend to be lower for the 4 and 5 hunger levels. This means that I tended to perform better on the aim speed test when I was less hungry, confirming my findings in project 2.

Time of day and aim speed

Bar chart showing the relationship between aim time and time of day.

This visualization shows the relationship between time of day and aim speed in milliseconds (ms). The time of day is split into 4 groups, Morning, Afternoon, Evening, and Late night. The data suggests that time of day can have an effect on aim speed test completion time. Although the difference isn’t very large, the tests completed during the afternoon tend to have the lowest completion times whereas the tests completed in the morning tend to have the highest completion times. This means I tended to perform better in the afternoon. This could be because the afternoon is when I am most awake or because it is when I would have eaten lunch which is usually quite a substantial meal. To get a better understanding on whether time of day has an influence of the aim speed I will look at aim speed based on the specific hours in which I completed a test.

Aim speed throughout the day

This visualization shows the relationship between aim speed in milliseconds (ms) and the time of day in more depth by using {lubridate} to get the specific hours of the day where I had filled out the form. The data suggests that aim speed varied depending on the hour the data was recorded. Earlier hours in the day generally had higher scores. Average aim times then appeared to improve around midday and later in the evening, where lower values were recorded. There are a few extreme points one in the morning at approximately 10 am where I was very slow to complete the test. This could be because I was at uni and using the track pad of my laptop. There us also an incredibly low recorded time at approximately 5 pm which could have been because there are not many values recorded at 5pm so the average is much lower than the other times.

Aim speed based off location

This visualization shows the relationship between aim speed in milliseconds (ms) and the location in which I filled out the form. The data doesn’t show a huge difference between any of the locations, however the bars for home and library are lower, with home being the lowest, whereas campus and public transport seem to be relatively similar to each other. This suggests that I tended to do slightly better on the aim speed test while I was at home than in the other locations I took the test. This could be because I was able to focus better while at home or in the library or because at home I would have my mouse, making it easier to aim whereas in the other locations I would be using a track pad or my phone.
